coredata.dc:description: Excessive accumulation of cadmium (Cd) impairs crop growth by inducing oxidative damage through the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). In this study, a biocompatible ferruginated carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) nanozyme is developed to target ROS, thereby reducing oxidative damage and improving the absorption and transfer of Cd ions in wheat. Notably, Fe-CQDs exhibit multi-enzyme activities mimicking peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), and superoxide dismutase (SOD), enabling effective neutralization of active species such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and superoxide anions (O2•−). Importantly, root application of 10 mg L−1 Fe-CQDs alleviates Cd stress and promotes wheat growth in both hydroponic and soil cultures. Specifically, the levels of O2•−, H2O2, and malondialdehyde (MDA) in leaf tissues decrease, whereas the non-enzyme antioxidant, reduced glutathione (GSH), increases. Cell wall thickness in the Fe-CQDs-treated group is reduced by 42.4% compared with the Cd group. Moreover, Fe-CQDs enhance the expression of genes related to antioxidants, stress resistance, Cd detoxification, and nutrient transport. Transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses show that Fe-CQDs stimulate the production of flavonoids and regulate the activity of metal transporter genes (YSL, ABC, ZIP) to maintain ROS homeostasis. These findings highlight the potential of Fe-CQDs nanozyme platforms in mitigating oxidative damage and enhancing crop growth, offering new insights into the application of nanobiotechnology in agriculture.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of Fe-CQDs root application on wheat seedlings. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean. Significant differences among treatments are indicated by different letters (P < 0.05, ANOVA with LSD, n = 3–6). (A) Comparison chart of wheat seedlings' morphology. (B) Fresh and dry biomass of wheat seedlings. (C) Photosynthesis parameters of wheat seedlings. (D) Fe and cadmium content in wheat seedlings.

Root application of Fe-CQDs induces a response in the antioxidant defense system of wheat seedlings. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean. Significant differences among treatments are indicated by different letters (P < 0.05, ANOVA with LSD, n = 3–6). (A) Fluorescence staining images and fluorescence intensity quantification results of probes PI and DCFH-DA. (B) Antioxidant enzyme activity in wheat seedlings (POD, SOD, CAT, and APX). (C, D) Two types of ROS content in wheat seedlings (O2 •−, and H2O2). (E) The content of MDA in wheat seedlings. (F) The content of glutathione in wheat seedlings.

Transport and distribution of Fe-CQDs in wheat seedlings. (A) Laser scanning confocal images of different tissues in wheat seedlings treated with root-applied Fe-CQDs. (B) TEM images of wheat seedlings cultured with Cd (a–c) and Cd + NMs (d–f). The selected areas marked by red rectangles in (e) indicate Fe-CQDs; (c) and (f) are locally enlarged images of the marked area. (C) SEM images of wheat root tissues. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean. Significant differences among treatments are indicated by different letters (P < 0.05, ANOVA with LSD, n = 6).

Transcriptomic analysis results of wheat. (A, B) Overall assessment of different groups in wheat root and shoot. (C) Multilayer enrichment histograms of KEGG enrichment between the Cd and Cd + NMs groups in wheat root and shoot. (D) Bubble map of KEGG enrichment comparing the Cd and Cd + NMs groups in wheat root and shoot (top 20). (E) Heatmap illustrating the clustering of entries with highly significant KEGG enrichment between the Cd and Cd + NMs groups in wheat root and shoot.

Metabolomics analysis of wheat. (A) Percentage circle chart depicting the classification of differential accumulation metabolites (DAMs) between the Cd group and Cd + NMs group in wheat root and shoot. (B) Bubble map illustrating DAMs KEGG enrichment comparing the Cd group and Cd + NMs group in wheat root and shoot (top 20). (C, D) Expression and correlation analysis of significantly enriched DAMs between the Cd group and Cd + NMs group in wheat root (solid blue line indicates positive correlation; purple dashed line indicates negative correlation). (E, F) Expression and correlation analysis of significantly enriched DAMs between the Cd group and Cd + NMs group in wheat shoot (solid blue line indicates positive correlation). (G) Stacked bar chart presenting DAMs content in different groups of wheat root and shoot.

The impact of Fe-CQDs on the size and weight of wheat grains in a greenhouse setting. (A, B) Comparison chart illustrating the size of wheat grains in the greenhouse. (C) Comparison of the thousand-grain weight in the greenhouse. (D–L) Results from micronutrient analysis of wheat grains. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean. Significant differences among treatments are indicated by different letters (P < 0.05, ANOVA with LSD, n = 3).
The regulatory mechanism of the Fe-CQDs nanozyme in wheat seedlings.

head.ce:title: Multi-activity ferruginated carbon quantum dots nanozyme improves wheat seedling growth and Cd tolerance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive accumulation of cadmium (Cd) impairs crop growth by inducing oxidative damage through the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). In this study, a biocompatible ferruginated carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) nanozyme is developed to target ROS, thereby reducing oxidative damage and improving the absorption and transfer of Cd ions in wheat. Notably, Fe-CQDs exhibit multi-enzyme activities mimicking peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), and superoxide dismutase (SOD), enabling effective neutralization of active species such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and superoxide anions (O2•−). Importantly, root application of 10 mg L−1 Fe-CQDs alleviates Cd stress and promotes wheat growth in both hydroponic and soil cultures. Specifically, the levels of O2•−, H2O2, and malondialdehyde (MDA) in leaf tissues decrease, whereas the non-enzyme antioxidant, reduced glutathione (GSH), increases. Cell wall thickness in the Fe-CQDs-treated group is reduced by 42.4% compared with the Cd group. Moreover, Fe-CQDs enhance the expression of genes related to antioxidants, stress resistance, Cd detoxification, and nutrient transport. Transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses show that Fe-CQDs stimulate the production of flavonoids and regulate the activity of metal transporter genes (YSL, ABC, ZIP) to maintain ROS homeostasis. These findings highlight the potential of Fe-CQDs nanozyme platforms in mitigating oxidative damage and enhancing crop growth, offering new insights into the application of nanobiotechnology in agriculture.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive accumulation of heavy metals in crops can disrupt physiological processes such as oxidative stress, genotoxicity, and programmed cellular death, ultimately negatively affecting crop growth and development [1,2]. Understanding the mechanisms of heavy metal absorption, transport, and toxicity in crops is essential to enhancing crop growth. Emerging evidence suggests that moderate environmental stress can promote plant growth [3], and plants can mitigate this stress through systemic acquired domestication and systemic acquired resistance strategies [4]. To adapt to various environmental changes, plants utilize a range of internal signals, including reactive oxygen species (ROS), phytohormones, and various signaling molecules, as well as their complex regulatory networks, which are involved in transduction processes associated with reprogramming transcriptomics, metabolomics, proteomics, and corresponding changes in physiological states [5]. ROS are recognized as crucial signaling molecules in plant responses to both biotic and abiotic stresses. Therefore, enhancing plant resistance by facilitating the rapid removal of ROS through the stimulation of ROS generation and the modulation of ROS equilibrium presents a promising strategy.

Traditional approaches to address oxidative stress in plants under Cd stress include cultivating Cd-tolerant crops, using artificial fertilizers to mitigate nutrient stress, and adjusting hormonal levels to bolster stress resistance. However, these methods can prove inefficient, costly, and potentially harmful to the environment [6,7]. In contrast, nanomaterials present a promising tool for improving crop production [8,9]. The application of nanotechnologies in fertilizers and insecticides holds significant potential for enhancing crop yields [10]. Wu et al. examined the physiological mechanisms through which nanomaterials improve plant salt tolerance, demonstrating that nanobiotechnology can enhance crop resistance and pave the way for innovative agricultural research [11]. Additionally, Xing et al. investigated the environmental impacts of engineered nanomaterials on agricultural applications, particularly focusing on the diverse interactions between nanomaterials and symbiotic bacteria in plants, thereby establishing a foundation for the application of nanomaterials in agriculture [12].

However, biosafety remains one of the foremost challenges associated with nanomaterials. Carbon-based nanomaterials are increasingly employed to enhance plant stress resistance and regulate growth through their antioxidant properties [13]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit improved photostability [14], increased quantum yield [15], enhanced biocompatibility, and reduced cytotoxicity, along with superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like enzyme activity and ROS-scavenging capabilities [16]. Studies have indicated that transition metal-doped carbon quantum dots (e.g., Fe, Zn, or Mn) can enable nanozymes to achieve high activity [17]. Although the precise structural and functional associations of nanoparticles are yet to be fully elucidated, it is evident that structural alterations can significantly influence their interactions within living organisms, including hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, molecular recognition, and redox reactions [18]. Additionally, the potential toxic effects of nanoparticles on plants, soil microorganisms, and even humans warrant careful consideration [19]. Furthermore, the appropriate application of nanomaterials to crops presents a technical challenge. Notably, the regulation of Cd tolerance in wheat through the use of engineered carbon quantum dots nanozymes has not been reported.

Iron (Fe) is a critical nutrient essential for plant growth [20], and plays a key role in vital physiological processes such as chlorophyll synthesis, respiration, protein synthesis, and redox reactions within plants [21]. Among these processes, the photosynthetic mechanism accounts for the largest portion of iron demand [22]. Consequently, the application of ferruginated carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) nanozyme (defined as NMs) may enhance antioxidant enzyme activity, improve photosynthetic efficiency, and promote organic matter synthesis by stimulating light responses and CO2 assimilation in plants.

This study investigates a modified ultra-small Fe-CQDs nanozyme that exhibits blue emission fluorescence and mimics peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), and SOD activities, effectively reducing excess ROS in wheat and enhancing growth. The Fe-CQDs nanozyme also promotes the expression of genes associated with antioxidant defense, heavy metal detoxification, nutrient transport, and stress resistance. Transcriptomics and metabolomics analyses reveal that Fe-CQDs stimulate significant flavonoid production to remove excess ROS and regulate genes encoding metal transporter proteins (YSL, ABC, and ZIP) to maintain trace element homeostasis. This approach not only broadens the potential applications of nanozymes in agriculture but also paves the way for a deeper understanding of the molecular mechanisms that mitigate reactive oxygen species and enhance crop growth under adverse conditions.

The 5.0 mmol of citric acid (C6H8O7), 5.0 mmol of ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), and 10.0 mmol of o-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2) were combined with 40 mL of deionized water and magnetically stirred until fully dissolved. The resulting mixture was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. Following heating, the mixture was filtered, centrifuged, dialyzed, and freeze-dried to obtain Fe-CQDs powder.

The crystalline structure of Fe-CQDs was characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, DX-2700, Dandong). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of Fe-CQDs were assessed using a nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer (DLS, Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, UK). The morphology, structure, and lattice spacing of Fe-CQDs were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JSM4800F, JEOL, Japan) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB-250Xi, China) was employed to analyze the valence states of the elements present. Additionally, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) and Raman spectroscopy were utilized for further examination of the chemical structures of the Fe-CQDs.

Four experimental groups were established: a blank control group (CK group), a Cd stress group (Cd group), a Cd stress group with Fe-CQDs material (Cd + NMs group), and a Fe-CQDs material group (NMs group). The Cd stress concentration was set at 30 μmol L−1, with CdCl2·2.5H2O used for treatment. The dosage of the Fe-CQDs material was maintained at 10 mg L−1 over a period of 14 d. Detailed descriptions of the hydroponic and soil culture experimental conditions, as well as omics and other experimental methodologies, can be found in the Supporting information. If there is no specific cultivation condition, it refers to hydroponic conditions.

We prepared two materials, CQDs and Fe-CQDs. Based on the material characterization results (Figs. S1, S2), the properties of the two materials are comparable; however, Fe-CQDs exhibit superior performance relative to the CQDs. HRTEM images reveal that Fe-CQDs are evenly distributed with a lattice spacing of 0.204 nm (Fig. S1A). Their hydrodynamic diameter is approximately 1.80 ± 0.25 nm (Fig. S1B), and they possess a zeta potential of −5.35 mV (Fig. S1C). The optimal excitation wavelength and emission wavelengths for Fe-CQDs are around 365 nm and 440 nm, respectively (Fig. S1D). Additionally, Fe-CQDs exhibit excellent water solubility, appearing colorless under natural light and blue under ultraviolet (UV) light. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1E) shows a peak at 2θ = 24°, corresponding to the (002) crystalline plane of carbon. FT-IR spectroscopy of Fe-CQDs (Fig. S1F) indicates the presence of several hydrophilic functional groups. Furthermore, the Raman spectrum (Fig. S1G) reveals two primary characteristic peaks at approximately 1300 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1, which are associated with vibrations induced by defects in the carbon structure and the vibrations of sp2-bonded carbon atoms. XPS analysis (Fig. S1H) detects four elements: C, N, O, and Fe, with atomic percentages of 35%, 18%, 29%, and 18%, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra (Figs. S1I, L) provide detailed insights into the bonds present in Fe-CQDs, including Fe-C, Fe-N, Fe-O, Fe 2p3/2, and Fe 2p1/2. Moreover, Fe-CQDs demonstrate high hydrophilicity (Fig. S3A) and exceptional dispersion stability (Fig. S3B). In summary, we successfully synthesized blue fluorescent Fe-CQDs characterized by negative charge, small particle size, high solubility, excellent stability, and affordability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs multi-enzyme mimicking activity for ROS scavenging
ce:section.ce:para: POD activity was assessed through the colorimetric reaction of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) with H2O2 [23] (Fig. S3C). Upon decomposition of H2O2, both Fe-CQDs, and CQDs can oxidize colorless TMB to form blue oxidized TMB (TMBox) [24,25], exhibiting an absorbance peak around 652 nm. The absorbance value for Fe-CQDs exceeds that of CQDs. In contrast, no color change was observed in the control group without H2O2 or when employing the CQDs and Fe-CQDs materials (Fig. S3C), indicating that Fe-CQDs demonstrate superior capability compared to CQDs in simulating the removal of H2O2 by POD.

SOD activity was evaluated using nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) in the presence of riboflavin, whereby O2 •− reacts with NBT to produce blue methylhydrazone following exposure to light, characterized by an absorption peak at approximately 600 nm (Fig. S3D). Notably, Fe-CQDs exhibited a greater inhibition effect on blue methylhydrazone formation compared to CQDs, suggesting that Fe-CQDs possess more effective SOD-like activity (Fig. S3D).

CAT-like activity of Fe-CQDs can be directly observed through the number of O2 bubbles produced. As expected, the introduction of Fe-CQDs significantly enhanced O2 bubble production compared to the CQDs and CK groups, validating the capacity of Fe-CQDs to mimic CAT in the removal of H2O2 (Fig. S3E).

Based on these findings, we confirmed that the “recovery strategy” of multi-enzyme activity in Fe-CQDs nanozyme exhibits remarkable efficacy in scavenging ROS through multiple pathways, thereby establishing a robust foundation for maintaining ROS balance in plants.

Prior to further investigations, the biocompatibility of Fe-CQDs nanozyme in plant applications was assessed through in vitro cytotoxicity studies using IPEC-J2 cells as a model (Fig. S4A, B). After a 24-hour co-incubation of IPEC-J2 cells with varying doses of Fe-CQDs and CQDs, no significant cytotoxic effects were observed, even at a concentration of 160 mg L−1, indicating that Fe-CQDs exhibit high biosafety. Additionally, earthworms, which are highly sensitive to toxic substances and commonly used as standard organisms for assessing the toxicity of soil contaminants [26,27], were employed in experiments to evaluate the acute toxicity of Fe-CQDs (Fig. S4C). The results demonstrated that Fe-CQDs nanozyme had no significant toxic effect on earthworms, affirming its biocompatibility and safety. Collectively, these findings indicate that Fe-CQDs nanozyme is environmentally friendly and free from toxic side effects, which holds significant potential for wheat applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs nanozyme promotes the growth and photosynthesis of wheat
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results of hydroponic pre-experiments, 10 mg L−1 Fe-CQDs nanozymes were shown to enhance wheat seedling growth, improve photosynthesis, and reduce oxidative stress (Fig. S5). Consequently, 10 mg L−1 Fe-CQDs were selected as the optimal concentration for root application in hydroponics. A blank control group and three experimental groups were established, with 30 µmol L−1 CdCl2·2.5H2O as the treatment concentration. The groups were designated as follows: CK group (blank control), Cd group (30 µmol L−1 CdCl2·2.5H2O), Cd + NMs group (30 µmol L−1 CdCl2·2.5 H2O + 10 mg L−1 Fe-CQDs), and NMs group (10 mg L−1 Fe-CQDs) to investigate Cd stress in wheat. A batch of wheat was initially cultivated under soil conditions. The experimental results (Fig. 1 A) indicated that Fe-CQDs nanozyme treatment promoted wheat growth and alleviated Cd toxicity. However, concerns regarding the reliability of root-related data arose due to the difficulty in detaching wheat root from the soil. Therefore, further Cd stress experiments were conducted under hydroponic conditions. In our findings, the root length and stem length of wheat treated solely with Fe-CQDs showed significant increases compared to the CK group (Figs. 1B, S6A). The growth rate and robustness indices in the Fe-CQDs group were slightly higher than in the CK group, with a 30.73% increase in root length. Moreover, wheat in the pure NMs treatment group exhibited noticeably greener leaves than in the CK group (Figs. 1B, S6B). As expected, Fe-CQDs nanozyme significantly promoted the growth of wheat in terms of fresh and dry biomass. Specifically, the fresh weight and dry weight of the aboveground parts increased by 6.47% and 4.0%, respectively, compared to the CK group. Under Cd stress, the Fe-CQDs treatment resulted in increases of 30.99% in fresh weight and 30.12% in dry weight (Fig. 1C). Additionally, the fresh weight and dry weight of underground parts increased by 11.24% and 15.79%, respectively, compared to the CK group, whereas the Fe-CQDs treatment group demonstrated increases of 167.13% in fresh weight and 160.0% in dry weight under Cd stress conditions (Fig. 1C).

The content of chlorophyll-a, chlorophyll-b, carotenoids, and total chlorophyll in wheat treated with Fe-CQDs is significantly higher than in the CK group. Additionally, the Cd + NMs group outperforms the Cd group, indicating that the Fe-CQDs nanozyme can effectively regulate plant photosynthesis and enhance the photosynthetic activity of wheat (Fig. S6C). Photosynthetic parameters serve as important indices of stress (Fig. S6D). The net photosynthetic rate of wheat leaves in the non-Cd group (pure NMs) is 19.27% higher than that of the CK group, whereas the Cd + NMs group exhibits a 77% increase compared to the Cd group. The transpiration rate in the NMs group is 10.51% higher than that of the CK group, whereas the Cd + NMs group shows an increase of 38.73% compared to the Cd group. Similarly, stomatal conductance in the NMs group increased by 19.14%, and that of the Cd + NMs group increased by 65.48% compared to the Cd group. Furthermore, the intercellular CO2 concentration decreased by 8.13% under stress-free conditions (NMs) compared to the CK group, and by 7.1% in the Cd + NMs group compared to the Cd group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs nanozyme alleviates Cd content in wheat seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Following the application of Fe-CQDs under Cd stress, the iron content in the above-ground portion of wheat increased by 9.51% compared to the Cd group. Significant increases compared to the CK group and the Cd + NMs group compared to the Cd group due to the absorption of Fe-CQDs by the wheat (Fig. 1D). Notably, Cd content in both the above-ground and underground parts of wheat in the Cd + NMs group decreased by 9.48% and 9.70%, respectively, when compared to the Cd group. A comparison of the Cd and Cd + NMs groups reveals that the Fe content in the Cd + NMs group is higher, whereas the Cd content is significantly lower than that in the Cd group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs nanozyme regulates root cell activity and homeostasis in wheat
ce:section.ce:para: To visually monitor the development of wheat root tips, we stained the dead and living cells of seedling root tips using PI and DCFH-DA probes, respectively (Fig. 2 A). Following treatment with 10 mg L−1 Fe-CQDs (Cd + NMs group), the average fluorescence intensity of dead root tip cells decreased by 32.61% compared to the Cd group. The results from the fluorescence probe staining indicated an improvement in the apoptosis rate of apical cells following NMs treatment. Additionally, based on the analysis of fluorescence staining data, the average fluorescence intensity of root tips in the Cd + NMs group was reduced by 17.73% compared to the Cd group, suggesting that ROS content in the root tips of wheat was alleviated. This finding further indicates that the application of Fe-CQDs does not negatively impact wheat health.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs nanozyme increases the antioxidant ability of wheat seedings
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to the CK group, the expression level of the antioxidant system in the NMs group is significantly reduced. However, following the intervention with Fe-CQDs, the expression level of the antioxidant system returned to a normal state. In comparison to the CK group, the activities of POD, SOD, and CAT are significantly elevated in the leaves of wheat under Cd stress, whereas the antioxidant capacity in the Cd + NMs group significantly decreased after the intervention with Fe-CQDs (Fig. 2B). Additionally, the content of ROS (O2 •− and H2O2) (Fig. 2C, D) and MDA, a product of membrane lipid peroxidation (Fig. 2E), were significantly reduced compared to the Cd group. Furthermore, the levels of the non-enzymatic antioxidant GSH (Fig. 2F) in both the Cd + NMs and NMs groups significantly increased compared to the Cd stress group. In addition, the expression levels of SOD, POD, and CAT are nearly normalized, whereas the GSH level is up-regulated by 1.250 times to maintain the antioxidant status, indicating a rapid and effective stress response to Fe-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transport and distribution of Fe-CQDs nanozyme in wheat tissues
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the uptake and transport of Fe-CQDs throughout the entire wheat plant, we conducted laser scanning confocal imaging of wheat plants, including the CK group (Fig. S7) and the Fe-CQDs treatment group (Fig. 3 A), showing the effects in dark field, bright field, and superimposed field. Upon excitation at 405 nm, luminescence signals were detected in the leaves, stems, and root of wheat following 14 d of Fe-CQDs treatment (Fig. 3A). Notably, the luminescent signal in the root xylem was the strongest, suggesting that the transport of Fe-CQDs may occur alongside the uptake of micronutrients from the nutrient solution. TEM and HRTEM were employed to explore the absorption of Fe-CQDs by the root, stem, and leaf cells of wheat. As expected, no significant spherical nanoparticles were detected either inside or outside the cells of the Cd group (Fig. 3B (a–c)). In contrast, the distribution of Fe-CQDs, either agglomerated or dispersed, could be observed in the interstitial spaces of root, stem, and leaf cells, as well as within the cells of the Cd + NMs group. TEM images of plants treated with 10 mg L−1 Fe-CQDs clearly display the presence of Fe-CQDs, with aggregation occurring in both the intercellular and intracellular spaces in wheat (Fig. 3B (d–f)). After absorption by the root, Cd enters the xylem through various pathways and subsequently undergoes translocation from root to stems to leaves. We examined the xylem of the root using biological scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The Cd group exhibited significant morphological changes (Fig. 3C); the cellular structure of the xylem was severely damaged, and the thickness of the cell wall increased by 111.43% compared to that of the CK group. Interestingly, the thickness of the cell wall in the Fe-CQDs treatment group was reduced by 42.4% compared to the Cd group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs nanozyme induce transcriptional changes in wheat seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Principal component analysis (PCA) results indicate that the transcriptome profiles of both root and leaf tissue fractions are significantly different and exhibit high biological reproducibility (Fig. 4 A(a), B(a)). To elucidate the changes that occur in wheat seedlings following Cd stress, we performed KEGG and GO enrichment analyses on differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between CK and Cd (Figs. S8–S10).

In the comparison between the Cd and Cd + NMs conditions for root tissues, a total of 4250 gene expression levels exhibited significant changes, with 1011 genes being significantly up-regulated and 3239 genes being significantly down-regulated (Fig. 4A (b,c)). The KEGG enrichment results indicated that the effects of Fe-CQDs on the wheat organism were primarily concentrated in nine categories, among which the global and overview maps categories, particularly for metabolic pathways and secondary metabolite synthesis, were most prominent (Fig. 4C left). The 15 most enriched items, including phenylpropanoid metabolism, phenylalanine, alpha-linolenic acid, betalain, linoleic acid, mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signaling, and ABC transporters et al. are annotated (Fig. 4D left). The heatmap of DEGs clusters demonstrates three key metabolic categories involving amino acids, lipids, terpenoids, and polyketides; DEGs related to the metabolism of these three substances were significantly down-regulated (Fig. 4E left). According to the GO enrichment results, the overall physiological status of wheat exhibited a notable downward trend, particularly in five aspects: stress, response to stimuli (GO: 0050896), signal transduction (GO: 0023052), immune system processes (GO: 0002376), transporter activity (GO: 0005215), and antioxidant activity (GO: 0016209) (Fig. S11).

Compared to wheat root tissues, a total of 717 genes exhibit significant changes in their expression levels in wheat shoot tissues, with 204 genes showing significant up-regulation and 513 genes demonstrating significant down-regulation (Fig. 4B (b, c)). KEGG enrichment analysis reveals that the effects of Fe-CQDs nanozyme on the wheat organism are primarily concentrated in 10 categories, among which secondary metabolite synthesis is most prominent in the global and overview maps categories (Fig. 4C right). The five entries with the most significant enrichment include alpha-linolenic acid metabolism, linoleic acid metabolism, monoterpene synthesis, secondary metabolite synthesis, and MAPK signaling, respectively (Fig. 4D right). The key compounds involved in these entries are categorized into two groups: lipid metabolism and terpenoids & polyketide metabolism. Additionally, a heat map of pathway-associated DEGs indicates a substantial down-regulation of DEGs related to the metabolism of these two categories (Fig. 4E right). GO annotation of DEGs in wheat shoot tissues reveals that the functional classifications and physiological changes of DEGs between shoot and root tissues are fundamentally similar (Fig. S12).

Furthermore, wheat root and shoot undergo a rapid MAPK cascade response following Cd stimulation, with Fe-CQDs treatment also exerting a significant effect on signaling within this pathway (Fig. S13). Compared to the root system, there are slightly fewer DEGs associated with the MAPK signaling pathway in shoot tissues, with most regulated genes focused on defense against external stressors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs nanozyme induces metabolism changes in wheat seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Through extensive targeted metabolomics, we performed multivariate supervised analysis (orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis, OPLS-DA) on the wheat root and leaf metabolomics datasets to assess the metabolic changes induced by Cd stress and Fe-CQDs application. The OPLS-DA scoring chart demonstrates significant separation between groups (Fig. S14A–D). The comparative analysis of CK and Cd treatments reveals the KEGG enrichment results for root tissues (Fig. S14A, C) and leaf tissues (Fig. S14B, D).

In a comparative analysis between the root Cd group and the Cd + NMs group, a total of 178 differentially expressed metabolites were identified. Of these metabolites, 98 exhibited increased levels whereas 80 displayed decreased levels (Fig. S14E, G). As illustrated in Fig. 5 A left, these metabolites predominantly include flavonoids (50), phenolic acids (33), lipids (23), alkaloids (18), amino acids and their derivatives (15), terpenes (6), organic acids (5), lignans and coumarins (3), nucleotides and their derivatives (2), quinones (1), and others (22). All differential accumulation metabolites (DAMs) were annotated with KEGG and MetMap pathways, followed by enrichment analysis to identify pathways with a higher concentration of these differential metabolites and their regulatory changes (Figs. 5B left , C, S16). All DAMs present in the significantly enriched pathways were screened using Pearson correlation analysis with a correlation threshold of 0.8 and a P-value threshold of 0.05. A correlation network diagram for the DAMs was constructed, highlighting the positive/negative correlations among the substances (Fig. 5D), which illustrates the interconnections between the physiological processes in wheat under Fe-CQDs regulation. Additionally, changes in these DAMs were investigated under different experimental conditions (Fig. 5G left). In line with previous research findings, wheat displayed contrasting regulatory responses to Cd exposure following treatment with Fe-CQDs, suggesting that Fe-CQDs significantly contribute to maintaining the steady-state balance in wheat.

A total of 141 differentially expressed metabolites were detected in the comparison between leaf samples treated with Cd and Cd + NMs. Among these metabolites, 42 showed increased expression, whereas 99 exhibited decreased expression (Fig. S14F, H). As illustrated in (Fig. 5A right), the primary DAMs included lipids (34), amino acids and their derivatives (26), flavonoids (23), phenolic acids (22), alkaloids (20), lignans and coumarins (7), nucleotides and their derivatives (2), organic acids (1), and others (6). All DAMs underwent KEGG and MetMap pathway annotation (Figs. 5B right, S17A, B), revealing significant enrichment in the flavone and flavonol biosynthesis pathways as well as linoleic acid metabolism. Notably, the content of flavonoids and lipid compounds increased significantly (Figs. 5E, S17C). The same analytical approach used for root tissue analysis was applied to assess the correlation network of DAMs in leaf fractions. Five substances were found to be positively correlated with one another (Fig. 5F), indicating the role of Fe-CQDs in regulating multiple physiological processes in wheat shoot. Additionally, changes in these DAMs were examined under varying experimental conditions, with wheat leaf tissues showing opposing regulatory trends after Fe-CQDs exposure, although to a lesser extent than those observed in root tissues (Fig. 5G right).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs nanozyme affect the quality of mature seeds in greenhouse
ce:section.ce:para: The metal residue and micronutrient content in mature wheat seeds serve as critical indicators for evaluation. We conducted a full-growing season wheat experiment in a greenhouse, demonstrating that the application of Fe-CQDs nanozyme stimulated wheat growth, resulting in noticeable increases in kernel size and weight at harvest compared to other groups (Fig. 6 A, B). The thousand-grain weight of wheat kernels increased by 18.68% under non-stress conditions and by 31.61% under Cd stress compared with CK group (Fig. 6C). The enhancing effect of Fe-CQDs nanozyme may be associated with improved photosynthetic efficiency. Under Cd stress, the root application of Fe-CQDs reduced Cd content in wheat grains by 5.67% compared to the CK group (Fig. 6D), alongside corresponding reductions in Ca and S content (Fig. 6J, L). Additionally, Fe content increased by 20.31% (Fig. 6E), matching the Fe levels observed in the CK group and compensating for the Fe loss induced by Cd stress. Moreover, Mn and Cu levels also exhibited certain increases (Fig. 6F, I). Conversely, Zn, and B contents did not show significant changes (Fig. 6H, K). These results suggest that the root application of Fe-CQDs may represent an effective strategy for regulating micronutrient content in crops. The multifunctionality of Fe-CQDs nanozyme highlights its considerable potential and value in agricultural production.

Fe-CQDs nanozyme possess several beneficial characteristics, including ultra-small size, blue fluorescence emission, hydrophilic surface chemical structure, and iron doping. These attributes facilitate the absorption and transport of nutrients in wheat, enhance photosynthetic efficiency, and aid in the removal of ROS, thereby alleviating oxidative stress. The application of Fe-CQDs significantly improves the growth and resistance of wheat seedlings to heavy metal Cd stress. The detailed mechanisms underlying these effects are discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs enhanced antioxidant defense to alleviate Cd content
ce:section.ce:para: The multi-enzyme mimicking properties of Fe-CQDs were assessed through their interaction with natural substrates. POD is a crucial antioxidant enzyme in plants that catalyzes the breakdown of H2O2 into H2O [9]. SOD is a key enzyme in regulating cellular oxidative stress and lipid metabolism, preventing lipid peroxidation by catalyzing the conversion of O2 •− into O2 and H2O2. CAT plays a vital role in H2O2 metabolism, accelerating its decomposition into H2O and O2, thus alleviating oxidative damage [28]. The engineered Fe-CQDs effectively mimic the activities of these three enzymes—POD, SOD, and CAT—by removing various ROS, including superoxide anions (O2 • −), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and alleviating oxidative damage.

In both the Cd group and the Cd + NMs group, the increased iron content and the significant reduction of Cd levels further validate the ability of Fe-CQDs to remove heavy metal Cd. This phenomenon may be attributed to the ability of toxic metals like Cd to enter plant cells via transporters that depend on essential cations such as iron or Mg [29]. Cd and Fe share transporter proteins in wheat, which may lead to competition between Fe3+/Fe2+ and Cd2+ for entry into the plant system. Elevated levels of iron signaling may inhibit the expression of metal transporter protein genes, thereby reducing Cd uptake and alleviating oxidative damage associated with Cd stress in wheat. Indeed, the introduction of Fe-CQDs can lower ROS levels and the extent of membrane lipid peroxidation in wheat, whereas also promoting the production of the endogenous antioxidant glutathione (GSH). Notably, Fe-CQDs create a more favorable environment for wheat growth, exhibiting enzyme-mimicking activity that supports the in vivo antioxidant system.

When wheat plants were subjected to low concentrations of Cd stress, their antioxidant defense system was activated, resulting in a notable increase in the levels of POD, SOD, and CAT enzymes. In contrast, the changes observed in ascorbate peroxidase (APX) enzyme activities were relatively minor. Following the application of Fe-CQDs, the activities of POD, SOD, and CAT enzymes decreased correspondingly. These findings suggest that Fe-CQDs can enhance the plant's antioxidant system, helping it to mitigate heavy metal stress and alleviate oxidative damage caused by Cd, ultimately reducing the stress on the organism's antioxidant defense mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs regulated the cell wall development of wheat seedlings to alleviate Cd stress
ce:section.ce:para: The root of wheat interact directly with the external environment, and the negative charge and small particle size of Fe-CQDs nanozyme facilitate their entry into the plant system. Considering that Fe-CQDs are applied to the root, they can be transported through the xylem from the root to the stems and leaves, where they can be effectively absorbed and utilized by wheat seedlings. The thickening of the wheat cell wall is one of the phenotypic responses to Cd stress; Fe-CQDs may induce alterations in specific secondary metabolites, thereby restoring normal cell wall thickness and alleviating stress levels. With an average diameter of less than 2 nm, Fe-CQDs are smaller than xylem conduits, allowing them to move freely in and out of these channels, in alignment with results obtained from previous laser scanning confocal imaging studies. The plant cell wall serves as a barrier, limiting the movement of external particles into the plant. It has been reported that the cell wall pore size is approximately 13 nm [30], indicating the cell wall's critical role in Cd [31]. Additionally, Fe-CQDs downregulate certain genes associated with cell wall development. Lignin, synthesized through phenylpropanoid biosynthetic pathways, is a major component of plant cell walls, consisting of phenolic substances that are covalently bonded to polysaccharides and proteins [32]. These downregulated metabolites contribute to cell wall construction and enhance the mechanical support of wheat plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe-CQDs induced the production of flavonoids and regulates the genes encoding metal transporters
ce:section.ce:para: The DEGs in the Cd and Cd + NMs groups were analyzed to elucidate the mechanism of action of Fe-CQDs in mitigating Cd toxicity in wheat. Initially, it is essential for plants to possess transporter proteins that facilitate the processes of uptake and transport [33]. Notably, three categories of membrane-distributed metal transporter proteins were identified in wheat root tissues [34] (Fig. S18A). The first class comprises yellow stripe-like (YSL) proteins, including YSL15 and ZmYS1, which are significantly up-regulated and function as Fe-phytosolic acid transporters. The second class consists of Zrt/Irt-like proteins (ZIP), such as IRT1 and ZIP10, which also exhibit significant up-regulation; these proteins function as Fe2+/Zn2+ regulated transporters. The third group includes the well-known ATP-binding cassette (ABC) proteins, which harness energy from ATP binding and hydrolysis to facilitate the transmembrane transport of various substrates. ABC transporter proteins play crucial roles in a range of cellular processes, including phytohormone transport, growth and differentiation, responses to biotic and abiotic stress, and the maintenance of redox homeostasis [35]. In response to heavy metal stress, transporters and ATPases in plant root are activated, utilizing energy-driven transport mechanisms to move metals to other parts of the plant or directly chelate metals to prevent the accumulation of toxic substances. Although Cd lacks a specific transporter in plants, it can nevertheless be absorbed and transported via divalent metal transporters with low specificity due to its chemical similarities to other metals, including Fe, Mg, Zn, and Cu [36]. Upon the addition of Fe-CQDs, wheat somatic cells can reduce Cd absorption by promoting iron uptake. Fig. S18B presents five proteins closely associated with heavy metal transport in wheat. These include metallothioneins (MT2C, MT1), heavy metal ATPase proteins (HMA9, HMA2), heavy metal-associated isoprenylated phycobiliproteins (HIP39, HIP23, etc.), copper transporter proteins (CCH), and vesicular membrane transporter proteins (VIT1). Vesicle regionalization serves as a mechanism for plant enrichment and Cd tolerance. The cell walls and vesicles act as passive areas that can retain and isolate heavy metals, thereby reducing their mobility [37]. Metallothioneins, which are low molecular weight proteins rich in cysteine and containing sulfhydryl groups, can bind to Cd, forming nearly non-toxic or non-toxic complexes within plant cytoplasm and vesicles, ultimately minimizing damage to organelles [38]. Cd ions entering the vesicles can also form macromolecular compounds through chelation with proteins, sugars, organic acids, and other organic compounds in the vesicles [37], further decreasing heavy metal toxicity to plants. Additionally, we identified a class of membrane-distributed efflux pumps (DTX), including DTX40, DTX16, and DTX27, which facilitate the efflux of Cd [39]. Furthermore, GSH S-transferase (GST) is recognized for its role in detoxification, reducing oxidative stress, and participating in phytohormone transport by binding to GSH [40]. Importantly, all GST-related genes exhibit significant up-regulation in wheat root tissues. Moreover, the introduction of Fe-CQDs nanozyme leads to the up-regulation of heat shock protein genes HSP16.9, HSP18.6, HSP18.9, and HSP26.2 (Fig. S18C). These genes regulate the activity of antioxidant enzymes, such as POD, SOD, and CAT, reducing the accumulation of ROS in the cells, eliminating harmful proteins, and maintaining normal cellular metabolism [41]. These findings further confirm the involvement of Fe-CQDs nanozyme in the regulation of the antioxidant defense mechanisms in wheat.

The application of Fe-CQDs significantly impacts the signal transduction of the MAPK pathway. Various signaling molecules interact, leading to progressive amplification and delivery to the cell via phosphorylation and dephosphorylation processes. These signals activate specific effector proteins, such as enzymes or transcription factors, in the cytoplasm or nucleus, thereby regulating the expression of particular genes [42]. In response to external stress, the MAP kinase substrate MKS1 activates the WRKY33 transcription factor and regulates camalexin synthesis. The response to such attacks involves transcription factors (VIP1, WRKY22/29), disease-associated proteins (PR1), and enzymes (ACS6). Furthermore, in response to external abiotic stress, Ca2+ signaling, in conjunction with calmodulin (CaM), regulates the expression of subsequent enzyme genes. The down-regulation of the protein kinase OXI1, which is tied to ROS homeostasis, suggests that intracellular ROS production and cell death in wheat cells are effectively suppressed, thereby maintaining ROS homeostasis. Additionally, the down-regulation of MKK9 and ERF1 affects the expression of ChiB, ultimately leading to reduced ethylene synthesis. Regulation of MYC2 by jasmonic acid (JA) promotes root growth and mitigates water deficiency caused by Cd toxicity in wheat, aligning with previous phenotypic observations. Concurrently, transcription factor families such as WRKY, MYB, AP2/ERF-ERF, bHLH, Tify, C2H2, and NAC play crucial roles in plants' responses to abiotic stress [43], with their transcripts being regulated in diverse manners. It is evident that the MAPK cascade response also influences the regulation of other secondary signaling pathways, including phytohormone signaling, highlighting the interconnectivity between these systems. The utilization of Fe-CQDs activates multiple hormone-mediated defense responses [44] and is closely associated with various significant enrichment pathways, including amino acid metabolism, lipid metabolism, and terpenoid and polyketide metabolism. Abscisic acid (ABA), JA, and salicylic acid (SA) are categorized as Cd stress-response hormones, whereas IAA, CTK, GA, ETH, and BR function as growth-promoting hormones (Fig. S19). Upon detection by the PYR/PYL receptor, ABA regulates stomatal closure by inhibiting the protein phosphatase PP2C, thereby enhancing wheat’s survival under adverse conditions. JA is associated with the alpha-linolenic acid metabolic pathway, which is catalyzed by the amino synthase JAR1, facilitating the formation of the COI1 complex. This process inhibits the ubiquitin-mediated degradation of JAZ proteins, releasing the transcription factor MYC2 in response to Cd stress, ultimately improving Cd tolerance in wheat by influencing the synthesis of monoterpenes and indole alkaloids. The phenylalanine metabolic pathway is associated with SA, which indirectly activates the regulatory protein NPR1, leading to the activation of the transcription factor TGA and expression of the pathogenesis-associated protein PR-1, which plays a vital role in enhancing disease resistance in wheat. Similarly, IAA, CTK, ETH, GA, and BR are associated with the metabolic pathways of tryptophan, cysteine, and methionine, as well as the synthesis of zeaxanthin, diterpenes, and oleoresin stanols. This alteration in hormone levels may represent one of the defense mechanisms by which plants perceive internal growth and developmental signals, as well as external environmental cues, helping to coordinate growth and development through the MAPK cascade in response to Cd stress.

Under the significantly enriched amino acid metabolic categories, an aromatic L-amino acid/L-tryptophan decarboxylase (DDC, TDC) co-regulates the metabolic network involving tyrosine, tryptophan, and phenylalanine, which are crucial for the synthesis of catecholamines and melatonin (Fig. S20). Tryptophan 5-hydroxylase (CYP71P1) and acetylserotonin O-methyltransferase (ASMT), unique to the tryptophan metabolic pathway, serve as key enzymes in melatonin synthesis. As a regulator of plant growth and development, as well as an effective stress-relief molecule, melatonin plays a vital role at both cellular and physiological levels, particularly in protecting plants from various stress conditions and effectively promoting plant growth [45]. Catecholamines scavenge ROS, defend against pathogens by participating in cell wall composition, enhance the plant's antioxidant properties, and improve adaptations to adverse conditions [46]. Lignin, synthesized through phenylpropanoid biosynthetic pathways, is a major component of plant cell walls, consisting of phenolic substances covalently bound to polysaccharides and proteins [32]. In these synthetic pathways, the gene expression of many key enzymes is down-regulated, as illustrated in their metabolic networks and expression patterns shown in Fig. S21, which align with previous SEM results. We speculate that catecholamines induced by Fe-CQDs may play a role in cell wall defense against Cd, in conjunction with certain other substances. With enhanced Cd tolerance in wheat, the defense mechanism of cell walls has evolved beyond mere thickening against heavy metals, reducing the urgency for lignin synthesis.

In leaf tissue, the down-regulated lipoxygenases (LOX2S, LOX1/5) in the lipid metabolic pathway can oxidize polyunsaturated fatty acids, such as linoleic and alpha-linolenic acids. Lipoxygenases (LOX2S, LOX1/5) and hydroperoxide dehydratase (AOS, CYP74A) serve as key enzymes in the biosynthesis of the plant hormone jasmonic acid. Moreover, previous reports indicate that fluorescent carbon quantum dots act as artificial antennas, enhancing light-trapping capacity and improving the photosynthetic efficiency of plants [47]. In this experiment, Fe-CQDs nanozyme can indeed influence the efficiency of photosynthesis to some extent. The expression of subunit PsaF in Photosystem I, subunit PetG in Cytochrome (Cyt) b6/f complex, and the three protein subunits (PsbR, PsbQ, and PsbY) in Photosystem II are also regulated [48].

To summarize, Fe-CQDs likely inhibit Cd uptake by modulating the activity of metal transporter proteins. Concurrently, they activate phytohormonal signals (JA, ABA, SA), transcription factors, and protein kinases that participate in the MAPK cascade reaction to regulate the synthesis of amino acids, lipids, terpenoids, and polyketides. Some of these secondary metabolites, such as melatonin and catecholamines, may enhance the body’s antioxidant defenses and provide synergistic protection against Cd stress through various mechanisms, thereby promoting the growth of wheat seedlings.

Regarding metabolic changes, the DAMs associated with the biosynthesis of chrysoeriol and luteolin aglycones are classified as flavonoids. These are upstream substances synthesized through the flavone and flavonol biosynthesis pathways, which form the foundation for the downstream production of chrysoeriol and luteolin [49]. Chrysoeriol is further derived from metabolites including Chrysoeriol-6-C-glucoside-4′-O-glucoside, Chrysoeriol-7,4′-di-O-glucoside, Chrysoeriol-7-O-(6′-acetyl)glucoside, Chrysoeriol-7-O-(6′-malonyl)glucoside, Chrysoeriol-7-O-Sophoroside-5-O-glucuronide, Chrysoeriol-7-O-glucoside, and Isoscoparin-2′-O-(6′-p-coumaroyl)-glucopyranoside. Luteolin is derived from Diosmetin (5,7,3′-Trihydroxy-4′-methoxyflavone)*, Isoorientin-7-O-glucoside*, Luteolin-6-C-arabinoside-7-O-glucoside, Luteolin-6-C-glucoside (Isoorientin), Nepetin-7-O-glucoside (Nepitrin), and Orientin-7-O-glucoside*. The introduction of Fe-CQDs appears to induce the upregulation of flavonoids synthesized through the mangiferolic acid pathway for ROS scavenging in cells, potentially serving as an effective strategy to alleviate plant stress.

Additionally, the benzoxazinoid biosynthesis pathway functions as a branch of various plant secondary metabolite biosynthesis, with related DAMs belonging to the alkaloid group. The content of 2,4-Dihydroxy-1,4-benzoxazin-3-one (DIBOA), DIMBOA glucoside, and oxindole varies significantly; an increase in the content of indolin-2-one within this pathway further contributes to the accumulation of DIMBOA-glucoside, potentially associated with the regulation of the genes BX6, BX8, and BX9. Benzoxazines, which are chemosensitizers secreted by the root of many graminaceous plants, have notably increased levels that assist wheat in resisting fungal and bacterial infections in the root whereas enhancing the defense system’s functionality [50]. We hypothesize that benzoxazines act as Cd chelators, reducing the rate of Cd uptake by sequestering it in wheat and thereby aiding in the development of tolerance in heavy metal environments.

Linoleic acid metabolism and the alpha-linolenic acid metabolism pathway-associated DAMs are categorized as lipids. Notable substances that are significantly down-regulated include 13(S)-HODE, 13(S)-Hydroxyoctadeca-9Z, 11E-dienoic acid, 9-Oxo-10E, 12Z-octadecadienoic acid, 9S-Hydroxy-10E, 12Z-octadecadienoic acid, Arachidonic Acid, 13S-Hydroxy-9Z, 11E, 15Z-octadecatrienoic acid, 17-Hydroxylinolenic acid, and 2R-hydroxy-9Z, 12Z, 15Z-octadecatrienoic acid. Previous studies have indicated that fluctuations in the relative proportions of lipid species are crucial for stress adaptation. Unsaturated fatty acids play a significant role in helping plants acclimatize to various environmental changes by influencing membrane fluidity and stability [51]. The aforementioned seven free fatty acids may reduce membrane fluidity, thereby maintaining a low signaling response that enhances Cd tolerance in wheat. Furthermore, Fe-CQDs have been shown to induce alpha-linolenic acid, 12-OPDA, and (−)-jasmonate, which are regulated within the alpha-linolenic acid metabolism pathway and are all associated with jasmonate synthesis. This implies that the phytohormone jasmonate may act as a signaling molecule produced as a result of stimulation by exogenous nanomaterials.

In light of these findings, the metabolomics results suggest that the establishment of Cd resistance in wheat facilitated by Fe-CQDs nanozyme is connected to the biosynthesis of flavonoids, benzoxazines, and lipids. The joint analysis of the transcriptome and metabolome revealed a more uniform enrichment of benzoxazine biosynthesis, linoleic acid metabolism, and alpha-linolenic acid metabolism pathways in root tissues (Figs. S22, S23). In leaf tissues, however, the pathways related to flavonoid and flavonol biosynthesis, linoleic acid metabolism, and GSH metabolism exhibited more intensive enrichment in differentially expressed genes (DEGs) and DAMs.

Taken together, we hypothesized that Fe-CQDs nanozyme may stimulate the production of jasmonic acid-based phytohormone signaling molecules (JA, ABA, SA, IAA, GA) in wheat under Cd stress. These hormonal signals interact with Ca2+ signals, amplifying the signals and transmitting them to the cells via MAPK phosphorylation. This process activates intracellular transcription factors (WRKY, MYB, bHLH, etc.) or enzymes, leading to the synthesis of heat shock proteins (HSP) and flavonoids, which help to reduce ROS levels and restore redox homeostasis. Additionally, certain plant secondary metabolites, such as melatonin and catecholamines, may impede Cd uptake by contributing to the composition of the cell wall. Moreover, root-secreted benzoxazines enhance the plant’s defense system by inhibiting bacterial and fungal infestation in the wheat root system. Furthermore, Fe-CQDs can help maintain the dynamic balance of various metal elements within the plant by regulating the expression of ABC, ZIP, and YSL genes associated with metal transporter proteins in the cell membrane. This regulation promotes the uptake of essential nutrients like Fe, Mg, Zn, Mn, and Cu whereas inhibiting the absorption of heavy metal Cd. The Cd that enters the wheat cells is chelated and transported through MT, HMA, HIP, and CCH proteins to different plant tissues, facilitating Cd sequestration and minimizing damage to organelles. Additionally, a membrane-associated efflux pump (DTX) aids in the exocytosis of heavy metal Cd. The combined action of these pathways enhances wheat's tolerance to heavy metal Cd (Fig. 7 ).

In summary, biocompatible Fe-CQDs nanozyme exhibit multiple antioxidant enzyme activities (POD, SOD, CAT) and can elevate the levels of the antioxidant GSH to mitigate key ROS-mediated oxidative damage, including DNA damage, lipid peroxidation, and photosynthesis inhibition. Furthermore, Fe-CQDs modulate the expression of various genes, including those responsible for antioxidant activity, heavy metal detoxification, nutrient transport, and stress resistance, thereby enhancing the stress resilience of wheat and promoting its growth and development. Fe-CQDs not only facilitate the efflux of Cd but also upregulate the synthesis of benzoxazines and flavonoids. The integration of transcriptomics and metabolomics can elucidate the molecular mechanisms underlying enhanced resistance. The application of Fe-CQDs to wheat has led to notable improvements in growth and Cd stress resistance, providing a potential reference for the utilization of agricultural nanobiotechnology.
